outbound_official.asof: 2018 (table labelled 'Tabla actualizada-uniformada 2018'; the PDF was republished by Periodico Cubano in 2021 from the original MINREX document titled 'Convenios sobre Exención de Visado a Ciudadanos Cubanos')

visa_free.name: Antigua and Barbuda
visa_free.notes: Agreement on abolition of visa for holders of valid national passports, all passport types, signed 19 Nov 1998.

visa_free.name: Barbados
visa_free.notes: Agreement on abolition of visa for holders of valid national passports - all passport types ('para todo tipo de pasaportes'), signed 19 Feb 1996.

visa_free.name: Belarus
visa_free.notes: Convention on visa-free travel for citizens of both countries, signed 1 Jul 1997, in force 10 Mar 1999. Explicitly includes Cuban citizens with ordinary passport (pasaporte ordinario).

visa_free.name: Dominica
visa_free.notes: Agreement on visa exemption for holders of valid national passports - all passport types, signed 23 Oct 2001. Excludes those seeking employment, permanent residence, or paid public entertainment exceeding 28 days.

visa_free.name: Grenada
visa_free.notes: Agreement on abolition of visa requirement for holders of valid national passports - all passport types ('para todo tipo de pasaportes'), signed 4 Feb 1999.

visa_free.name: Guyana
visa_free.notes: Bilateral agreement covers diplomatic/official/service passports; however, Guyana unilaterally extends visa-free entry to Cuban ordinary passport holders ('Unilateralmente Guyana exime de visado a los portadores de pasaportes ordinarios cubanos').

visa_free.name: Indonesia
visa_free.notes: Unilateral Indonesian decision (Presidential Regulation No. 21 of 2016, in force 10 Mar 2016) exempting 169 countries including Cuba from visa requirement for 30 days. Not extendable; not applicable for journalism; passport must have 6+ months validity. No bilateral agreement in force.

visa_free.name: Iran
visa_free.notes: Unilateral Iranian decision taken in 2015 to exempt Cuban ordinary passport holders from visa for 30 days, extendable by 15 days. Separate bilateral MoU (Feb 2017) covers diplomatic/official/service passports only.

visa_free.name: Malaysia
visa_free.notes: Agreement on visa exemption for holders of valid national passports (all passport types), signed 26 Sep 1997, in force 26 May 1998. Covers tourism, family visits, business negotiations, investments, sport, seminars/conferences.

visa_free.name: North Korea
visa_free.notes: Agreement on reciprocal travel of citizens, signed 9 Jul 1996, in force 12 Feb 1997. Cuban side covers: diplomatic, official, service, ordinary, marine passports and identity/travel certificates. Duration: 'for the entire duration of the mission'.

visa_free.name: Russia
visa_free.notes: Convention on conditions of reciprocal travel, signed 3 Dec 1993, in force 29 Jul 1994. Article 1: citizens holding valid national passports may enter and remain for up to 30 days without visa. Excludes those intending to work, conduct individual business, or reside permanently (Article 3).

visa_free.name: Saint Kitts and Nevis
visa_free.notes: Agreement on abolition of visa requirement for holders of valid national passports, all passport types, signed 30 Mar 1998.

visa_free.name: Saint Lucia
visa_free.notes: Agreement on abolition of visa requirement for holders of valid national passports - all passport types ('para todo tipo de pasaportes'), signed 23 Jan 1999, in force 10 Jul 2000.

visa_free.name: Saint Vincent and the Grenadines
visa_free.notes: Agreement on abolition of visa requirement for holders of valid national passports, all passport types, signed 8 Jul 1997.

visa_free.name: Singapore
visa_free.notes: Agreement on reciprocal visa exemption for diplomatic, official, service and ordinary ('corrientes') passports, signed 22 Jun 2015, in force 31 Mar 2016. Ordinary passports covered for tourism or commercial activities for 30 days.

visa_free.name: Trinidad and Tobago
visa_free.notes: Original bilateral agreement (14 Oct 1997) covered diplomatic/official/service passports. On 1 Oct 2007, Trinidad and Tobago unilaterally eliminated the visa requirement for ALL Cuban passport types for visits not exceeding 90 days.

visa_on_arrival.name: Zimbabwe
visa_on_arrival.notes: Zimbabwe declared unilaterally on 3 Mar 2016 that Cuban citizens may purchase a category B visa at the port of entry, so no prior visa is required before departure from Cuba.

outbound_official.notes: Source is the official MINREX (Cuban Ministry of Foreign Affairs) document 'Convenios sobre Exención de Visado a Ciudadanos Cubanos - Tabla actualizada-uniformada 2018', republished as a PDF. The document is internally marked as the 2018 updated table and notes it is the version that superseded the 2019 update. The live MINREX servers (cubaminrex.cu and misiones.cubaminrex.cu) were unreachable at time of research (June 2026); the document was retrieved via a third-party mirror (periodicocubano.com) but its content is verbatim MINREX text. Countries where the bilateral agreement covers only diplomatic/official/service passports on the Cuban side are NOT listed here as visa-free for ordinary passport holders; examples include Mongolia (Cuban side: diplomatic/official/service/marine only), Kenya (ordinary exemption applies only to Kenyan citizens visiting Cuba, not Cubans visiting Kenya), China (2024 update added ordinary passports per Wikipedia but 2018 MINREX table only covers diplomatic/service/official on the Cuban side), and most African nations (diplomatic/official/service only). The 2019 MINREX update document (cubaminrex.cu/sites/default/files/2019-09/Convenios%20sobre%20Exenci%C3%B3n%20de%20Visado%202019.doc) was inaccessible; post-2018 new agreements (e.g., China 2024 ordinary passport update) are not captured in this source.

visa_free.notes: Ordinary passport holders exempt from visa requirement. Bilateral visa exemption agreement signed with Cuba, effective May 17, 2024 per Wikipedia. Excludes Hong Kong and Macau SAR passports.
Malaysian citizens do not need a visa to travel to Cuba as tourists; may remain in Cuban territory up to 3 months without applying for a visa.
Visa-free for ordinary passport holders; 90 days within any 180-day period. Bilateral visa exemption agreement with Cuba.
UAE citizens do not need to obtain a visa before traveling to Cuba. Confirmed by UAE Ministry of Foreign Affairs official page for the UAE Embassy in Havana.
Agreement on reciprocal exemption from visa requirement for holders of diplomatic, official, service and ordinary passports for tourism or commercial activities for 30 days; signed June 22, 2015.
Visa-free for ordinary passport holders for up to 30 days.
Visa-free for ordinary passport holders for up to 28 days.
Citizens of Dominica are visa-free for up to 28 days.
Visa-free for ordinary passport holders for up to 60 days.
Citizens of Saint Vincent and the Grenadines are visa-free for up to 60 days.
Visa-free for ordinary passport holders for up to 30 days.
Visa-free for ordinary passport holders for up to 30 days.
Citizens of Saint Kitts and Nevis are visa-free for up to 30 days.

e_visa.notes: Since August 2024, visitors from most countries may obtain an e-Visa for 90 days via evisacuba.cu. Extension possible for another 90 days once in Cuba (180 days total). The e-Visa replaced the physical tourist card from July 1, 2025. Applicants receive an alphanumeric code by email and must complete the D'Viajeros digital declaration form (dviajeros.mitrans.gob.cu) no later than 3 days before departure. The e-Visa is valid for 12 months from issuance, single entry. Excludes consulate-required nationalities and visa-free nationalities listed above.

visa_policy.notes: From July 1, 2025, Cuba ended the use of physical tourist cards; all international travelers must obtain an e-Visa before departure through the official portal evisacuba.cu - EXCEPT nationals who hold a bilateral visa-free agreement with Cuba (who may enter without any visa). The following nationalities MUST apply for a Tourist Visa A1 directly from a Cuban consulate (cannot use the e-Visa portal): Afghanistan, Bangladesh, Cameroon, Eritrea, Ethiopia, Ghana, Guinea, Haiti, India, Iraq, Iran, Kenya, Nepal, Nigeria, Pakistan, Philippines, Sierra Leone, Somalia, Sri Lanka, Syria, Uzbekistan, and Yemen. Kosovo nationals are refused entry and transit. All visitors must carry a valid return ticket, medical insurance proof (explicitly covering Cuba), and passport validity of at least 6 months from arrival; financial solvency of minimum USD 50 per day must be demonstrated. Mongolia, Namibia, Bosnia and Herzegovina, Montenegro, North Macedonia, and Serbia have historical bilateral agreements with Cuba but appear to now require a Cuba e-Visa under the post-July 2025 system rather than maintaining unconditional visa-free entry - this reclassification cannot be confirmed from a primary official MINREX source.

conditional_access.conditions: Cuba and Fiji signed an Agreement on Visa Exemption covering holders of diplomatic and official passports. Duration and ordinary passport coverage not confirmed from official sources.

cbi.notes: Cuba does not offer a Citizenship-by-Investment program. No official Cuban government source references any CBI scheme. The new Laws 171, 172, 173 (approved July 2024, published May 2026) expand residency criteria but do not establish a CBI program. Decree-Law 117/2026 creates an 'Investors and Businesspersons' immigration status, but this is targeted at Cuban citizens living abroad (the diaspora), not foreign nationals seeking citizenship.

programs.name: Permanent Residency for Foreign Nationals (Investment/Economic Ties Pathway)
programs.notes: Under Law 172 (Aliens Act, approved July 2024, effective ~November 2026), foreign nationals may obtain permanent residency on grounds including: significant assets or bank accounts in Cuba, foreign families with employment or economic ties to Cuba, 5+ years of residence demonstrating deep ties, advanced degrees or expertise in science/sports/culture/arts, or as parents of Cuban-born citizens with actual Cuban residence. No minimum investment amount is stated verbatim in the official source. Also introduces 'provisional residence' as a pathway to citizenship. The new law also recognizes 'investment capacity' as grounds for residency.

visa_types.name: Tourist Visa A1 (e-Visa / Electronic Tourist Visa)
visa_types.purpose: Short-term tourism, leisure, and visiting family. From July 1, 2025, replaces the physical tourist card; all visitors not covered by a bilateral visa-free agreement must obtain this before departure.
visa_types.notes: Obtained via evisacuba.cu; applicant receives an alphanumeric code by email. Passport must be valid for at least 6 months from arrival. Visitors must also complete the D'Viajeros digital declaration form (dviajeros.mitrans.gob.cu) no later than 3 days before departure, carry proof of medical insurance covering Cuba, a valid return ticket, and demonstrate financial solvency of at least USD 50 per day. Extension for an additional 90 days (180 days total) can be requested inside Cuba. Certain nationalities (Afghanistan, Bangladesh, Cameroon, Eritrea, Ethiopia, Ghana, Guinea, Haiti, India, Iraq, Iran, Kenya, Nepal, Nigeria, Pakistan, Philippines, Sierra Leone, Somalia, Sri Lanka, Syria, Uzbekistan, Yemen) cannot use the online portal and must apply for the Tourist Visa A1 directly from a Cuban consulate.

visa_types.name: Tourist Visa A1 (Consulate-Issued)
visa_types.purpose: Short-term tourism for nationalities excluded from the e-Visa portal. Issued at Cuban embassies and consulates abroad.
visa_types.notes: Required for nationals of: Afghanistan, Bangladesh, Cameroon, Eritrea, Ethiopia, Ghana, Guinea, Haiti, India, Iraq, Iran, Kenya, Nepal, Nigeria, Pakistan, Philippines, Sierra Leone, Somalia, Sri Lanka, Syria, Uzbekistan, and Yemen. These nationalities cannot use the evisacuba.cu portal and must appear in person at a Cuban diplomatic mission. Same entry requirements apply (medical insurance, D'Viajeros form, return ticket, financial solvency).

visa_types.name: Business Visa / Non-Tourist Visa (D Category)
visa_types.purpose: For foreign nationals traveling to Cuba for business activities, commercial negotiations, investment, or participation in trade events.
visa_types.notes: Issued by Cuban embassies and consulates. Applicants typically require an invitation letter from a Cuban state entity or authorized Cuban business partner. The new Law 172 (Aliens Act, in force ~November 2026) introduces an 'investor and businessperson' immigration status but it is primarily aimed at Cuban diaspora; foreign nationals with business ties may apply for residency under the investment/economic ties pathway. Specific fee schedule was not accessible from official sites at the time of research.

visa_types.name: Work Visa / Labor Authorization (Category C)
visa_types.purpose: Issued to foreign nationals contracted to work in Cuba for Cuban state entities, joint ventures, or authorized foreign companies operating in Cuba.
visa_types.notes: Requires a labor contract with a Cuban state entity or an authorized employer. The employing entity typically initiates the process with Cuban immigration authorities (MININT). Under Law 172 (Aliens Act), foreigners with employment ties to Cuba may qualify for provisional or permanent residency after five years of continuous residence. Cuba does not have an open private labor market; all work authorization flows through state-sanctioned channels.

visa_types.name: Student Visa (Category E)
visa_types.purpose: For foreign nationals enrolled in accredited Cuban universities, medical schools, or other educational institutions.
visa_types.notes: Requires an official enrollment letter from an accredited Cuban educational institution. Cuba historically accepts international students on scholarship programs (Latin American School of Medicine - ELAM is a well-known example). Visa validity generally aligns with the duration of the academic program. Holders of student visas must register with immigration authorities upon arrival.

visa_types.name: Diplomatic / Official Visa
visa_types.purpose: For accredited diplomats, government officials, and staff of international organizations with official missions in Cuba.
visa_types.notes: Issued through diplomatic channels. Holders of diplomatic or official passports from countries with bilateral visa-exemption agreements covering diplomatic/official/service passports enter Cuba without a visa. Countries with diplomatic/official-only visa waivers (not covering ordinary passports) include Fiji (signed bilateral agreement) and various others listed in the 2018 MINREX Convenios PDF.

visa_types.name: Transit Visa
visa_types.purpose: For passengers transiting through Cuba en route to a third country without passing through immigration controls.
visa_types.notes: Required for nationals who do not benefit from visa-free transit. Passengers remaining airside (not clearing immigration) generally do not require a transit visa, but this depends on nationality and airline policy. Passengers who must clear Cuban immigration (e.g., due to a flight connection requiring terminal change) need a transit visa issued by a Cuban consulate. Kosovo nationals are refused transit.

visa_types.name: Family Reunification / Residency for Foreign Spouses and Relatives
visa_types.purpose: Allows foreign nationals who are spouses, parents, or dependants of Cuban citizens to reside in Cuba.
visa_types.notes: Under Law 172 (Aliens Act, approved July 2024, in force ~November 2026), foreign nationals who are parents of Cuban-born citizens with actual Cuban residence are explicitly eligible for permanent residency. Foreign families with employment, economic, or survival ties to Cuba are also recognized. Provisional residence is available as a step before applying for permanent residency or citizenship. Applications handled by MININT (Ministry of the Interior).

visa_types.name: Investor / Businessperson Immigration Status
visa_types.purpose: Special immigration status for foreign nationals (and Cuban diaspora) with significant investment activity or assets in Cuba, established under Decree-Law 117/2026.
visa_types.notes: Created by Decree-Law 117/2026, effective immediately upon publication in May 2026. Grants a specific immigration status to investors and businesspersons. Although initially framed around Cuban diaspora, the Granma article confirms it applies to those with 'significant assets or bank accounts in Cuba' as a basis for residency - which extends to foreign nationals. No minimum investment threshold was published in the official source. This is a residency-track status, not a citizenship-by-investment program.

visa_types.name: Provisional Residence
visa_types.purpose: A temporary residency status for foreign nationals establishing ties with Cuba, serving as a prerequisite for applying for permanent residency and eventually citizenship.
visa_types.notes: Introduced under Law 172 (Aliens Act, approved July 2024). Provisional residence is a recognized pathway before permanent residency. The law also introduces protections for vulnerable foreign nationals (victims of armed conflicts, insolvent tourists) - an implicit humanitarian stay category. Applications processed through MININT.

visa_types.name: Medical / Health Tourism Visa
visa_types.purpose: For foreign nationals traveling to Cuba to receive medical treatment at Cuban healthcare facilities.
visa_types.notes: Cuba has a well-established medical tourism sector (operated through Servimed/Salud Cuba). Foreign patients typically enter on a tourist visa but may be issued a dedicated medical visa category when extended treatment is required. Specific procedural details were not accessible from official MINREX sources during research due to server inaccessibility; this category is confirmed to exist in practice through Cuba's international health cooperation programs.

validation_notes: ADDED: UAE (Emirati, ARE) as visa-free 90 days - confirmed by UAE Ministry of Foreign Affairs official page (mofa.gov.ae/en/missions/havana). Previous record listed UAE as a gap/unconfirmed entry.

REMOVED: Mongolian (MNG) from visa_free list - reclassified. Multiple aggregator sources indicate Mongolia, along with Bosnia, Montenegro, North Macedonia, and Serbia, are now required to obtain a Cuba e-Visa under the post-July 2025 system despite prior bilateral agreements. Wikipedia's Visa policy of Cuba page (current as of June 2026) does not list Mongolia in its visa-free table. Moved context to notes.

REMOVED: Namibian (NAM) from visa_free list - same reasoning as Mongolia; prior record already noted duration was unconfirmed, and current aggregator data places Namibia in the 'previously visa-free, now needs e-visa' group. Moved context to notes.

CORRECTED: Notes section now includes India and Iran in the consulate-required (A1 Tourist Visa) list - official MINREX/misiones.cubaminrex.cu source explicitly lists both India and Iran alongside Afghanistan, Bangladesh, Cameroon, etc.

CORRECTED: Kenya is NOT visa-free - official MINREX source (misiones.cubaminrex.cu) explicitly lists Kenya in the consulate-required Tourist Visa A1 nationalities. Prior record's gap note about Kenya being potentially visa-free per Wikipedia was incorrect; MINREX source takes precedence.
CLARIFIED: Haiti confirmed as consulate-only (Tourist Visa A1 required); retained in notes section.

NOTED: Mongolia, Namibia, Bosnia & Herzegovina, Montenegro, North Macedonia, Serbia status ambiguity documented in notes - these countries have historical bilateral agreements but appear to require e-visa under the new system; cannot fully confirm from a direct MINREX source due to server accessibility issues.

RE-VERIFIED 2026-06-27: 13 visa-free nationalities (CHN 90d, MYS 90d, RUS 90d, ARE 90d, SGP 30d, BLR 30d, BRB 28d, DMA 28d, GRD 60d, VCT 60d, ATG 30d, LCA 30d, KNA 30d) confirmed consistent with Wikipedia 'Visa policy of Cuba' (fetched 2026-06-27). cubaminrex.cu and misiones.cubaminrex.cu servers remain inaccessible (malformed HTTP response). Russia 90-day stay confirmed by multiple sources (updated from the 1993 bilateral 30-day figure). UAE visa-free confirmed again by UAE MOFA official page (mofa.gov.ae); stay duration of 90 days remains unconfirmed in official text - retained from previous run citing Wikipedia/aggregator consensus.

RE-VERIFIED 2026-06-27: Serbia - Cuba-Serbia visa exemption for ORDINARY passports was explicitly CANCELLED on 13 April 2023 (confirmed by Wikipedia citing directoriocubano.info news report on the June 2023 Cuba-Serbia diplomatic agreement; that 2023 agreement restored visa-free access ONLY for diplomatic and official passports). Serbia is correctly excluded from visa_free.

RE-VERIFIED 2026-06-27: Montenegro - Montenegro reinstated a visa regime with Cuba (gov.me official diplomatic page does not reference a Cuba ordinary-passport visa-free regime). Montenegro correctly excluded from visa_free.

RE-VERIFIED 2026-06-27: Bosnia, North Macedonia - Timatic data cited by Wikipedia shows these citizens may technically be visa-free per old bilateral agreements, but the Cuba e-visa portal (evisacuba.cu) reportedly requires them to obtain an e-visa. Cannot confirm either way from a primary MINREX official page due to server inaccessibility. Keeping excluded from visa_free (classify down per safety rule) with gap noted.

RE-VERIFIED 2026-06-27: Iran - confirmed still requires consulate-issued visa (e-visa NOT available) by embassies.net Cuba-Iran page and hotelhavanaerror.com visa guide; correctly retained in consulate-required list in notes.

RE-VERIFIED 2026-06-27: India - non-official sources (hotelhavanaerror.com, joinsherpa.com) suggest India may now be e-visa eligible ('new 2026') rather than requiring a consulate visa. Cannot confirm from official MINREX source (servers inaccessible). India classification unchanged (consulate-required) per safety rule; added to gaps for follow-up.

RE-VERIFIED 2026-06-29: All 13 visa-free nationalities (CHN, MYS, RUS, ARE, SGP, BLR, BRB, DMA, GRD, VCT, ATG, LCA, KNA) remain unchanged. Wikipedia 'Visa policy of Cuba' page re-confirmed the same 13-country list. cubaminrex.cu and misiones.cubaminrex.cu servers remain inaccessible (malformed HTTP response); evisacuba.cu returns browser compatibility warning. No new visa-free agreements found.

RE-VERIFIED 2026-06-29: Serbia ordinary passport status re-confirmed via official Serbian Ministry of Foreign Affairs page (mfa.gov.rs): 'For holders of national passports and other travel documents: Visas required.' Diplomatic/official passports only are exempt for 90 days. Serbia correctly excluded from visa_free. cubavisa.uk incorrectly lists Serbia as visa-free; Serbian MFA is the authoritative source.

RE-VERIFIED 2026-06-29: UAE visa-free confirmed again by UAE MOFA official page (mofa.gov.ae/en/missions/havana). Max stay still not stated on official page; 90 days supported by embassies.net (non-official). Retained as 90 days with gap note.

RE-VERIFIED 2026-06-29: India - additional non-official sources (visago.dev, itzeazy.in, fareeagle.com, entrycubaform.com, atlys.com, joinsherpa.com, akbartravels.com) all indicate India IS now e-visa eligible via evisacuba.cu for 2026, with no consulate requirement. This is strong convergent evidence but still not confirmed from official MINREX page (inaccessible). India classification remains consulate-required per safety rule; gap note strengthened.

RE-VERIFIED 2026-06-29: Bosnia and Herzegovina, North Macedonia - status remains ambiguous. Timatic reportedly shows visa-free per old bilateral agreements; cubavisa.uk and cubavisa.uk/countries-exempt list them as visa-free (Bosnia 90d, North Macedonia 90d). Cannot confirm from MINREX. Keeping excluded per safety rule (classify down).

RE-VERIFIED 2026-06-29: Mongolia, Namibia - cubavisa.uk lists Mongolia as 30d visa-free, Namibia with unspecified duration. Wikipedia does not include them in the visa-free table. Cannot confirm from MINREX. Keeping excluded per safety rule.
gaps: The 2018 MINREX Visa Waiver Agreements PDF (cubaminrex.cu) could not be directly fetched due to server errors; some visa-free nationality entries rely on that document being cited in search results rather than direct scraping.
The Fiji-Cuba visa exemption agreement details (ordinary passport coverage and stay duration) are not confirmed from the official page, which returns an HTTP error.

Mongolia, Namibia, Bosnia and Herzegovina, Montenegro, North Macedonia: historical bilateral agreements are documented in the 2018 MINREX PDF but their current post-July 2025 status (visa-free vs. e-visa required) cannot be definitively confirmed from a primary MINREX announcement. Multiple aggregators (cubavisa.uk) classify Bosnia (90d) and North Macedonia (90d) as visa-free; Wikipedia does not include them. Serbia's ordinary-passport exemption was explicitly cancelled April 2023 and confirmed by Serbian MFA. Montenegro reinstated a visa regime. Bosnia and North Macedonia remain ambiguous per safety rule.

Exact stay duration for UAE ordinary passport holders in Cuba is not stated verbatim in the UAE MOFA source; 90 days cited by embassies.net and Wikipedia. The UAE MOFA Havana page (fetched 2026-06-27 and 2026-06-29) confirms visa-free access but does not specify a maximum stay.

No official Cuban government page listing the complete and current visa-free country list was directly accessible due to server/SSL errors on cubaminrex.cu and misiones.cubaminrex.cu (persists as of 2026-06-29).
Cuba has no digital nomad visa or fast-track skilled worker program - confirmed absence from search results, but no official government denial page was found.
Minimum investment amount for residency under the new Law 172 criteria is not stated in the official Granma article; it refers to 'significant assets or bank accounts' without a numeric threshold.

CONDITIONAL ACCESS (Type A - held third-country credential): No official rule found granting visa-free, VoA, e-visa, or eTA entry to Cuba based on holding a valid foreign visa (US, Schengen, UK, Canada, etc.) or residence permit. Cuba does not appear to operate a credential-based conditional entry pathway.

CONDITIONAL ACCESS (Type C - diplomatic/service passport bilateral waivers): The 2018 MINREX PDF likely contains a full list, but was inaccessible. Diplomatic-only waivers for additional countries cannot be confirmed without reading the PDF directly.
Kosovo entry refusal: confirmed by Wikipedia and aggregators but not confirmed by a primary official MINREX page.

India consulate-vs-evisa classification: As of 2026-06-29, a large number of independent non-official travel sources (joinsherpa.com, visago.dev, itzeazy.in, fareeagle.com, entrycubaform.com, atlys.com, akbartravels.com) all describe India as now eligible for Cuba e-visa via evisacuba.cu. The MINREX official page (misiones.cubaminrex.cu) listing India as consulate-required was fetched and confirmed in a prior run but the server is now inaccessible (malformed HTTP response) and cannot be re-fetched. Current classification (consulate-required) retained per safety rule; this item is HIGH PRIORITY for re-verification against official MINREX source when servers become accessible.

Russia stay duration: current record shows 90 days; the original 1993 Cuba-Russia bilateral agreement cited in the MINREX 2018 PDF stated 30 days. Multiple current sources (Wikipedia, embassies.net) confirm 90 days for 2025-2026. An updated Cuba-Russia agreement superseding the 1993 treaty on duration is assumed but not directly cited from a Cuban official page.
